content:
14 min read
Just now

--

It felt faster to handle the rule directly in the UI, until every new change had to untangle duplicated logic, hidden API assumptions, inconsistent state, and behavior nobody clearly owned.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The frontend shortcut that hurt the most did not look dangerous at first.
It was small.
A condition near a button.
A quick status mapping inside a screen.
A permission check added before rendering an action.
A response cleanup step before showing a table.
A temporary form exception that everyone agreed to revisit later.
Each change made sense in isolation.
Then the same product rule appeared in five places, and a simple change became a search through screens, helpers, API assumptions, cached data, and duplicated UI behavior.
The interface still looked clean.
The responsibility was scattered.
That is what makes this shortcut expensive. It rarely feels like bad engineering when it happens. It feels practical. The screen needs to work. The user needs a correct view. The release is close. The backend change may take longer. The API response is already available in the frontend. So someone says the sentence that quietly changes the shape of the codebase.
“Just handle it in the UI for now.”
Sometimes that is the right tradeoff.
The problem is not that frontend code has logic. Every serious frontend has logic. A real interface must handle interaction, display states, validation feedback, loading behavior, formatting, accessibility, empty states, and errors. A frontend with no logic is usually not a product. It is a screenshot.
The problem starts when the UI becomes the place where unclear ownership, weak contracts, inconsistent data, and temporary product exceptions go to hide.
The shortcut does not remove complexity.
It moves complexity into the part of the system users touch most.

The Shortcut Was “Just Handle It in the UI”
A single UI condition is not the problem.
Most products need them. You might hide an action when the user cannot perform it. You might show a warning when a payment failed. You might disable a field until another field is selected. You might map an internal status to a label users can understand.
That is normal frontend work.
The problem starts when every unclear rule gets solved at the screen level because the screen is the fastest place to make the problem disappear.
The API returns something inconsistent, so the UI patches it. A permission rule is not fully documented, so one page hides the button. A form has one customer-specific exception, so a condition goes near the input. A dashboard needs a slightly different label, so the view maps the backend status locally.
None of these decisions feels reckless in the moment.
They feel efficient.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The team is not trying to create a mess. They are trying to ship. They are trying to avoid blocking on another team. They are trying to keep the interface usable. The shortcut is often chosen by reasonable developers under reasonable pressure.
But architecture is not only shaped by big decisions. It is shaped by repeated local decisions that become normal.
Once “handle it in the UI” becomes the default response, the frontend slowly becomes a storage area for unresolved product meaning. Rules that should have been clarified become conditions. API inconsistencies become helper functions. Permissions become display checks. Validation becomes duplicated field logic. Business decisions become scattered rendering branches.
The codebase still works.
But change starts getting slower.
Not because the frontend has too much code, but because the frontend now owns decisions it was only supposed to display.
The UI Started Fixing Weak API Contracts
One of the fastest ways to make frontend code harder to change is to let every screen interpret the backend differently.
At first, this usually begins with small inconsistencies.
One endpoint returns status. Another returns success. Another returns isActive. One error is a string. Another error is an object. One empty array means there is no data. Another empty array means the user does not have permission. A failed request sometimes means the server failed, and sometimes means the user needs to upgrade their plan.
The frontend can patch all of this.
That is exactly why the problem spreads.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A table maps ACTIVE to “Active.” A details page maps active to “Enabled.” A dashboard treats missing data as zero. A form treats missing data as not configured. A report screen treats the same absence as an error. None of these differences look huge during implementation, but they create a system where every screen speaks a slightly different language.
The dangerous part is that the user experience may still look polished. The table renders. The dashboard loads. The form submits. The error message appears. But underneath, the frontend has stopped consuming a contract and started inventing meaning.
A response like this looks simple:
{
"status": "failed",
"message": "Payment could not be completed",
"data": null
}But if one screen treats failed as a retryable payment issue, another treats it as a blocked account, and another only checks whether data is null, the problem is no longer the response shape. The problem is that the meaning of the response lives in too many places.
This is where debugging becomes slow.
A user reports that failed payments show differently on two pages. The backend says it returned the same status. The frontend says both screens are working as designed. Product says the label should be consistent. Nobody is obviously wrong, but nobody knows where the truth lives.
The better judgment is not “put no API logic in the frontend.”
The better judgment is: do not let every screen invent the meaning of the backend response.
A healthy frontend can normalize data at the boundary. It can define shared response handling. It can translate backend values into product-facing UI concepts in one clear place. The exact pattern matters less than the ownership. What matters is that screens should not each become their own private interpreter of the API.
When every view has its own translation layer, the API contract is no longer a contract.
It is a suggestion.
Permissions Became Display Logic Instead of Product Rules
Permissions are one of the most tempting places to use this shortcut.
The frontend already knows the current user. The screen already knows which button is being rendered. Hiding or disabling the action feels simple. It also improves the experience. A user should not see actions they cannot perform. A button that will fail anyway should not look available.
That part is fine.
The problem begins when the frontend becomes the only place where the permission rule is understood.
One screen hides “Delete” for a support role. Another screen forgets the check. A bulk action uses a different condition. An export button checks the subscription plan but not the role. A route guard blocks one path, but a direct API call still succeeds. The backend protects some actions but assumes the UI will protect others.
Now permission behavior depends on which part of the product the user reaches.
That is not only messy. It is dangerous.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Frontend permission checks are important for guidance, clarity, and user experience. They help avoid showing impossible actions. They reduce confusion. They make the product feel responsive and intentional. But frontend checks should reflect permission truth, not become the source of it.
The difference matters.
If the backend owns the rule and the frontend reflects it, the system has a clear authority. The UI can still hide buttons, disable fields, and explain limitations, but it is not inventing access control locally. If the rule changes, there is a clear place to update it and a clear contract for the UI to consume.
If every screen owns its own version, the rule becomes behavior by accident.
This creates painful change later. Product decides that managers can export reports but cannot delete records. That sounds like a small permission update. In a well-owned system, the frontend adjusts how it reflects the new permission model. In a scattered system, the team searches through buttons, menus, route guards, tables, modals, and old helper functions looking for every place where someone added a local role check.
The bug is not always that unauthorized users get access.
Sometimes the bug is that authorized users cannot find the thing they are allowed to do.
Sometimes one page says yes, another says no, and support has to explain behavior the system itself cannot explain.
The UI should make permissions understandable.
It should not be the only place where permissions become true.
Data Transformation Spread Across Screens
Frontend work naturally includes transformation.
Raw data is rarely the same shape as useful interface data. Dates need formatting. Prices need currency rules. Names need fallback behavior. Empty values need display decisions. Backend statuses need user-facing labels. Lists need sorting, grouping, filtering, or derived fields.
There is nothing wrong with that.
The problem is transformation without ownership.
It often begins as a display detail. A table needs to show “Inactive” instead of 0. A dashboard needs to group failed and cancelled payments together. A profile page needs to show “Not invited yet” when invitedAt is missing. A billing screen needs to round numbers differently from the report export.
Each transformation makes sense where it is written.
Then the product changes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The word “Inactive” should become “Disabled.” Failed and cancelled payments should no longer be grouped. Missing invitation dates should show a different message for admins. Currency rounding should follow the account region instead of the browser locale. Suddenly, a simple display change becomes a codebase search.
The team looks through tables, cards, modals, filters, export handlers, format helpers, and old conditional rendering. Some transformations are inside reusable utilities. Some are inline inside screens. Some are hidden inside state preparation. Some happen after fetching. Some happen before rendering. Some happen again before submitting.
This is how the frontend becomes difficult to change without looking obviously bad.
The problem is not that the UI transforms data. The problem is that the same product meaning gets transformed in different places with different assumptions.
A label is not always just a label. A formatted status often carries business meaning. A rounded number might affect user trust. A date display might decide whether something looks expired, pending, overdue, or complete. When that meaning is scattered, the product becomes inconsistent even if each screen is technically correct.
The better approach is not to centralize everything blindly. Some transformations are genuinely screen-specific. A compact table and a detailed invoice may display the same value differently for good reasons.
But those reasons should be intentional.
A useful question is simple: if this value changes meaning tomorrow, where should someone look first?
If the answer is “probably every screen that touches it,” the frontend has already made future change harder.
State Became a Patch for Unclear Behavior
Frontend state can solve real problems.
It manages interaction. It tracks open menus, selected rows, dirty forms, filters, drafts, optimistic updates, pagination, and temporary user choices. Without state, modern interfaces would feel broken.
But state also becomes a convenient place to hide uncertainty.
A loading flag covers a slow or unclear request lifecycle. A local copy of server data covers the fact that the refresh strategy is missing. A cached response covers repeated calls, but nobody knows when it becomes stale. A selected
